Sweden · a scaling law

Sweden · a scaling law

City and Village
Petrol Stations of Sweden

City and Village
Petrol Stations of Sweden

Petrol stations are the nodes of the car–transport network — fuel bought to enable commutes, vacations, and the haulage of goods. More people mean more demand for all of it. But does more demand mean more stations?

Petrol stations are the nodes of the car–transport network — fuel bought to enable commutes, vacations, and the haulage of goods. More people mean more demand for all of it. But does more demand mean more stations?

Yes — though in more interesting ways than you might guess. Out of a competitive, heterogeneous market a remarkable regularity emerges. A scaling law: one compact rule for how an economy presses against the land, be it in big cities, in small settlements, out in the forest, and up at the northern ski resorts.

Yes — though in more interesting ways than you might guess. Out of a competitive, heterogeneous market a remarkable regularity emerges. A scaling law: one compact rule for how an economy presses against the land, be it in big cities, in small settlements, out in the forest, and up at the northern ski resorts.

3,263
petrol stations, the whole country
290
municipalities — and ~6,200 census tiles when we zoom in
β 0.59
one exponent, the whole story

Five ways to cut up Sweden

The economics of place needs a geographic unit to measure in — and everything that follows hangs on which one. Here are five ways to cut the land into units, drawn one inside the next. Scroll, and watch the country divide.

Definitions follow Statistics Sweden’s regional divisions.

land municipality · 290 RegSO · ~3,400 DeSO · ~6,200 settlement (tätort)

One country

450,000 km², most of it forest and lakes, the population pressed into a few percent of the land.

290 municipalities

The administrative units. They cover the whole country with no gaps, each typically centred on a town or city and its surrounding landscape.

~3,400 RegSO

Every municipality divides further into RegSO — named regions of roughly 700 to 23,000 residents, their borders drawn to follow natural boundaries like roads and waterways. The mesh tightens, finest where the people are.

~6,200 DeSO — zoom in

Push in on Norrköping and Linköping. Every RegSO splits once more into DeSO — the finest mesh, again shaped around clusters of land use and the lie of the terrain. Note what holds these steady: not area, but people. Each one is drawn to hold roughly 700 to 3,000 residents, so the tiles are large out in the forest and tiny in the cities. Remember that; it matters later.

Settlements

Now the built-up areas bloom on top — the cities and towns. Drawn around buildings rather than the mesh, they spill across the DeSO lines. They cover just ~2% of the land, yet ~88% of Swedes live inside them; everywhere else is tiny villages or remote and rural.

People and Stations Scaling

Take every Swedish municipality. Plot along the bottom the number of people living there (S); plot up the side how many petrol stations it has (P). If stations simply tracked people, the dots in the scatter plot would fall along a line.

They don’t.

Linear axes

Each step adds

Equal gaps mean equal amounts: every gridline is another +250,000 residents. The cloud climbs fast for small towns, then sags — the big cities fall well below a straight ramp. Your eye says it levels off?

Log–log axes

Each step multiplies

Now stretch both axes so equal gaps mean equal factors: every gridline is another ×10. The bend pulls straight — and a straight line on log–log paper is a power law, its whole story in one slope, β = 0.59.

What is a scaling law?

$$P = C\,S^{\beta}$$

The constant $C$ sets the overall rate; the exponent $\beta$ sets how the count grows with size. Read it off by doubling — take a municipality twice the size, $S_{d}=2S$:

$$P_{d} = C\,S_{d}^{\beta} = C\,(2S)^{\beta} = 2^{\beta}\,C\,S^{\beta} = 2^{\beta}\,P$$

So the single factor $2^{\beta}$ decides everything:

  • $\beta > 1$ — superlinear: doubling the people more than doubles the stations
  • $\beta = 1$ — linear: stations track people one‑for‑one
  • $\beta < 1$ — sublinear: doubling the people less than doubles the stations

Sweden’s municipalities sit firmly in the last case: at β = 0.59 petrol stations scale sublinearly with population — the bend you just watched straighten, written as one number.

Where the dots come from — sources, cleaning, and what got excluded

Stations are OpenStreetMap amenity=fuel (Geofabrik extract, June 2026): 3,421 raw features, deduplicated at 50 m to 3,263 stations. That sits sensibly above the industry association’s ~2,700 staffed-and-automat count, because OSM also carries marina pumps, truck stops and depot pumps — so for the car-fuel analyses we scope out 7 marine/harbour pumps and 68 pure gas/biogas sites, leaving 3,188. Nothing is deleted: every station keeps its raw tags plus a transform trail, and 283 mis-filed operator brands were folded back in from name fields before any brand analysis.

People and places come from Statistics Sweden: settlement (tätort) boundaries and populations 2023, the DeSO/RegSO census meshes, and municipal populations (LAU 2024). Cars are registered vehicles per municipality, Trafikanalys 2025. All slopes in this piece are fitted with Poisson / Negative-Binomial count models — why that matters is its own drawer, a few scrolls down.

The big picture

Bigger places run leaner

Double a municipality’s population and its petrol stations grow by only about 20.59 ≈ 1.5× — a pattern that holds across the country’s 290 municipalities.

There is a plausible economics to it. A forecourt is a lump of fixed costs — land, tanks, canopy. As towns grow, businesses can usually serve more people by expanding existing stations than by building new ones. Competition for valuable land and improvements in transport networks reinforce the same tendency, favouring fewer but busier forecourts.

Other urban-scaling studies find exactly this signature for infrastructure of many kinds, with exponents in the 0.60–0.90 band (for a popular account, see Scale (2018) by Geoffrey West).

So much for the description. Now the hard part — whether the number means anything at all.

“City people just own fewer cars” — true, and it explains about a fifth of it

A station serves cars, not residents, and city-dwellers own fewer. Count each municipality’s registered cars instead of its people and the slope moves — then stalls. Cars-per-person really do fall with size (0.60 in small municipalities, 0.49 in large), so roughly a fifth of the gap was car ownership. But β climbs only from 0.59 to 0.68, well short of 1. Even counted per car, big places are served more efficiently. Granted in full; the effect survives.

The hinge

“You drew the geographic boundaries wrong”

There is something arbitrary in any demarcation of geography. A municipality is a political demarcation, not an economic one. What if we clustered people and petrol stations differently? This problem is sometimes called the Modifiable Areal Unit Problem.

One complication is that a municipality almost always bundles both a town and its rural surroundings — and for the purposes of transport, these are different worlds glued into one box. Really, the choice of unit is one dial: how big a box to draw. Turned coarse, a box is a whole municipality, town and hinterland together; turned toward fine, it is a tight built-up settlement and nothing else.

So we redo the regression, counting only people and stations inside settlements — drop the hinterland, keep the town. The slope is then 0.78: still sublinear, though markedly less so — larger settlements are provisioned more efficiently than smaller ones, same as before, the box just drawn tighter. Redraw it a few more ways and the pattern holds. Here is every version so far at once, each with its uncertainty, all measured against the wall.

And the honest answer to the objection is: yes, partly. That spread from 0.59 to 0.81 is real, and no amount of arguing makes it go away. Which unit is right?

Sweden’s population is not spread out — it is pressed into a few percent of the land, with everything else close to empty. So a municipality is almost never one thing: it is a dense core plus a rural hinterland, glued into a single dot. If β = 0.59 is really an average of two different regimes, it is not trustworthy at all — just a mixture that happens to come out tidy.

There is only one way to find out. Unblend it.

How we measured the slope honestly — count models and kept zeros

Petrol stations are counted, not measured, and many small settlements have zero, one, or two. The textbook move — take logs and fit a straight line — cannot take the log of zero, so it silently drops every stationless place and biases the slope upward (for settlements, from 0.78 to a fake 0.46). We instead fit a Poisson / Negative-Binomial count model with a log link, which keeps the zeros and treats a town going from one station to two as the ordinary high-variance wobble it is, not a doubling.

Act III · the interrogation

Zoom in, and one law becomes two

Sundsvall shows the problem cleanly — a compact city of ~46,000 wrapped in 3,400 km² of forest, one dot on every chart so far, two fuel economies on the ground. Sundbyberg is the opposite pole: 56,000 people on nine square kilometres of unbroken city, and two stations. Blend those in different proportions across 290 municipalities and any slope you fit is a weighted average of something.

So take the DeSO mesh from the opening map — ~6,200 tiles of 700–2,700 residents each, fine enough that most are close to internally uniform. And because “rural versus urban” is at bottom a statement about density, put density straight on the x-axis.

There it is. Two regimes, and the data prefers them decisively. Below the breakpoint provision grows as fast as density — no economy of scale in the countryside at all. Only above it does urban efficiency kick in. The tidy 0.59 really was an average of two different worlds, and we have just caught it.

A satisfying result. It is also wrong, and the next chart is the first hint of why. Push past the breakpoint, reading the same tiles without a hinge:

91% of the very densest tiles (above 50 ppl/ha) contain zero stations. Read straight: more people, fewer pumps — the dense city core as a fuel desert, a market running backwards.

What produces “the local slope” — segmented regression, plus a model-free check

The two headline numbers — 0.98 below the break, 0.26 above — come from a segmented (breakpoint) count regression: a Poisson/Negative-Binomial GLM with a hinge in log-density, fit with an area offset so it reads population density directly. The breakpoint itself is chosen by a grid search over candidate values and picked on AIC (segmented beats a single slope decisively, ΔAIC ≈ 415). That model returns exactly two slopes, one on each side of one hinge — by construction it cannot show anything past the break except a flat 0.26.

The curve above is a different, more agnostic object: at each of the sixteen density bins, the local slope is the raw numerical derivative of log(station density) with respect to log(population density) between neighbouring bins — no functional form imposed, no assumption that there are only two regimes. It agrees with the segmented model where the two should agree, then goes on to show what a two-slope model structurally can’t: the slope doesn’t just flatten in the densest tiles, it keeps falling, through zero. That decline is the fuel-desert signature the next section explains.

No market runs backwards. So it is not the market that broke — it is the ruler.

The turn

The ruler shrank

Here is the thing about the DeSO mesh, hiding in plain sight since the opening map. Those tiles are drawn to hold roughly constant population — 700 to 2,700 people each. So as density rises, the tile does not stay the same size and fill up. It shrinks.

A rural tile is 11 km². A tile in the densest tenth of the country is half a square kilometre — about 180 metres from centre to edge. Meanwhile the ground one petrol station actually serves does not shrink at all: people drive five or ten kilometres for fuel whether they live in Stockholm or Sorsele.

That gives a test you can apply before fitting anything at all: counting stations inside a box only works when the box is bigger than the ground a station serves. Check the boxes against that rule and the whole earlier wander falls into place — the municipality passes and reads 0.59; the settlement and the dense tile fail, and read 0.78 and worse. The units did not disagree at random. They disagreed by exactly how badly they broke the rule.

Two ways to break a ruler — leakage, and the settlement’s different disease

Leakage is what happens to a dense DeSO tile: it is far smaller than a catchment, so the station its residents use sits in the tile next door and gets counted against someone else. Demand and supply are sliced along different lines.

The settlement (tätort) fails differently. It is small too, but its real problem is that it is not a partition of the country at all — it is built-up land only. Drawing the box around the buildings drops the hinterland whose residents drive in to fill up, so the town keeps its pumps but loses a chunk of the people they serve. That is selection, not leakage, and it pushes the slope the other way — which is why tätort reads high (0.78) rather than collapsing.

The fix

Stop drawing boxes. Count who can drive there.

Land costs the most exactly where the tiles are smallest, so pumps quietly relocate to the arterials and ring roads a kilometre or two off — from those “empty” tiles the nearest station is only about 0.7 km away, in the next tile over. Nothing is missing; it is just filed next door. So drop the boxes and draw a catchment instead: stand at a tile and count every station within driving range, and every person too. Drag the radius and watch two slopes — a tile’s own residents (the wobbly one) against its whole catchment (the steady one).

How far will people drive for fuel?10 km
slope — residents inside the tile
0.85
peaks, then collapses — an artefact of the ruler
slope — residents within driving range
0.60
holds near 0.59 at every radius

The catchment slope barely twitches — 0.59 ± 0.02 from 2 km all the way to 50 km — and it lands exactly on the municipality’s 0.59 from the very first chart. The humble municipality was the trustworthy ruler all along, and now we know why rather than by luck: a municipality is about catchment-sized, big enough to hold a town together with the countryside that fuels around it.

Which leaves one thing unfinished. What happened to the two regimes?

The reversal

Both halves were wrong

The countryside and the city were measured with the same broken ruler, so ask the question again with the good one: fit the catchment slope separately for rural and urban tiles, and widen the driving range.

Read the hollow circles on the left edge: that is what the box method claimed. Now read where the lines actually go. The countryside was never near-linear — the rural slope never rises above 0.73 at any driving range, and settles at 0.61. And the city was never flat at 0.26; it comes back to 0.58.

The box did not find two regimes. It took one law and bent it in two directions at once — flattering the countryside toward proportionality, crushing the city toward nothing.

That is the whole story of the middle of this piece. The rural–urban split was never a fact about Sweden; it was a fact about the mesh. Measure demand and supply over the same ground, and there is one exponent — the same 0.59 the 290 municipalities gave up in the very first chart, before any of this began.

Why the countryside looked linear — the indivisible first station

At a 5 km range, 27% of rural tiles have no station within reach and 47% have at most one. Down there provision is quantised: a village either clears the threshold that justifies a single pump or it does not. One station, then two — against a population that doubled — reads as near-proportional growth, because you cannot build half a forecourt. That is what manufactured the 0.98. Widen the range until the counts fill in (a median of 19 stations at 20 km) and the rural slope sinks to 0.61 with the rest.

One honest caveat: this uses a single driving range for both regimes, and rural Swedes plausibly drive further than urban ones. Pairing each with its own range would reopen a gap — though in the opposite direction to the one the box method claimed. What survives every choice is the headline: nothing here is near-linear.

Every problem in this act was one problem, seen from the dial’s two ends. Coarse blends — a municipality averages town and forest into one dot, and only an extreme mix like Sundsvall betrays it. Fine fragments — a tile shrinks below a single station’s reach, its pumps land next door, and the dense core reads as a desert. The catchment escapes because it is not another box: it is a window the real size of demand, and it can overlap its neighbours. Count people and pumps over the same patch of ground, and the law reads 0.59 from the densest block of Stockholm to the empty north.

Why this isn’t just “another unit” — the slope is invariant on two axes at once

A catchment is a moving window, not a partition — neighbouring windows overlap and share stations — and the Modifiable Areal Unit Problem is a disease of partitions. The exponent holds across every travel radius and across the underlying grain (DeSO ≈ RegSO ≈ municipality ≈ 0.59), which is why we trust it. (Overlap does make the confidence interval look tighter than it is; honest error bars need a spatial block-bootstrap — flagged, not yet run.)

The pivot

Now trust the line — and study what misses it

The slope survived the ruler that nearly broke it — so we stop measuring it, and start using it.

The fitted law becomes a quiet baseline; the dots that sit off it become the subject. A municipality with far more stations than its residents can explain is being asked a question: how big would you have to be to justify these pumps? Run the law backwards and the answer is the town’s effective population — the crowd of non-residents the pumps are really serving.

$$S_{\text{eff}}=\left(\tfrac{P}{C}\right)^{1/\beta}$$

The size that would make the observed station count most likely. The gap $S_{\text{eff}}-S$ is the implied crowd of ghosts: tourists, cross-border shoppers, transit traffic — demand the resident count never saw.

This is the real payoff of a scaling law: not the description, the baseline. One caution travels with it: Sundsvall has already shown that a dot can miss the line for reasons of supply-side geography — forest, not phantoms — so each residual is a question to investigate, not a verdict. What follows is the lens at work — three quick looks, a tasting menu rather than a census. Where the ghosts gather; what explains them; and who serves the thin places the law barely reaches.

Is the baseline good enough to trust? — the count model, the fan, and the rootogram

Worth pausing before we lean on it. On a log axis the settlement cloud fans out alarmingly at the small towns — a jump from one station to two looks as violent as twenty to forty. In a straight-line-through-logs frame that fanning would sink the model. But we never fit a straight line to logs; we fit a count model, and a count model predicts precisely this shape: the wobble around a place that expects μ stations grows only like √μ, so it looks enormous at the bottom of the range and vanishes at the top. The real test is whether the spread the model actually draws is the spread it claims — and it is, running if anything a touch tighter than a plain Poisson would allow, which is why no heavier-tailed model was needed here.

Through the lens · one

Ghosts — and where they gather

Slide a point horizontally to the baseline and read off where it lands. The distance is the surplus or missing demand, now in plain population units.

MunicipalityResidentsStationsFuels like
Malung-Sälen10,26017~55,300×5.4 ghosts
Härjedalen10,14515~44,700×4.4
Tingsryd (transit + visitors)12,15516~49,900×4.1
Sundbyberg55,9122~1,500×0.03
Nacka110,63310~22,600×0.20

A positive gap is unseen inbound demand; a negative gap is residents fuelling elsewhere. And the gaps are not scattered at random. Put every municipality where it actually sits, and the ghosts arrange themselves into geography: over-stationed places line the Norwegian mountain border and the sparse north; under-stationed places ring the Stockholm and Göteborg suburbs.

Is any single town really an outlier? — no; the signal is the clustering

Said plainly: no single municipality is a defensible outlier. Fourteen stray past the 95% mark — against 14.5 expected by pure chance — and none survives a multiple-testing correction. Rare things happen. What cannot happen by chance is the arrangement: the surprises clump hard (Moran’s I = 0.42, p = 0.001, a ~13σ departure from randomness). The structure is in the geography, never in any one dot — which is why the next scene interrogates regions, not towns.

Through the lens · two

Tourists — but only the kind that drive

If the extra stations serve visitors, the visitors should leave a footprint. Not the walk-around kind — a crowd at Stockholm’s Old Town arrives by metro and buys no fuel. The tourism that moves petrol arrives by car and stays: a ski week in Sälen, a camping trip on Öland. So we built the proxy deliberately from overnight beds and ski-lifts — and ignored the urban kind.

It lands: even after stripping out residents, their cars and their income, a municipality’s leftover surplus still follows this mobile-tourism proxy at +0.43. Better, the lens can pull tangled stories apart. The Norwegian-border municipalities are over-stationed and they are the big ski destinations — switch the tourism proxy on, and watch it separate them:

Municipalitybefore tourismafter tourismwhat the lens says
Årez 1.08z −0.41pure ski resort — fully explained
Älvdalenz 1.13z 0.00fully explained
Härjedalenz 2.41z 1.08half ski, half border
Malung-Sälenz 3.28z 1.62ski and cross-border fuel

Pure ski resorts collapse to zero — the surplus was the skiers. The border-and-ski towns keep a stubborn residual: tourism erases about 57% of the Norway over-stationing, a significant remainder survives, and the Finnish border shows neither effect. That remainder is genuine cross-border fuel arbitrage — Norwegians driving over to fill up where tax is lower. One residual surface, two mechanisms, separated.

What the lens still can’t see — the Småland puzzle, and income

The motorway cluster. With the mountains explained by tourism, the largest unexplained over-stationed group is interior Småland — Växjö, Alvesta, Ljungby. Not border, not high-tourism, and surviving cars, income and tourism. The likely culprit is through-traffic: the E4 and routes 25/27 carry freight and pass-through demand that neither resident cars nor overnight stays capture. Because tourism has already ruled out the obvious alternative, this is the clean next thing to test (Trafikverket traffic counts).

Income is a bundle, not a mechanism. The wealthy Stockholm-ring municipalities have fewer stations even per car, and income absorbs a quarter of that deficit — but income is inseparable here from land price and density, which is the very economics of sublinear scaling. A strong association; not proof that wealth zones out pumps.

Through the lens · three

Out in the thin tail, the big brands quietly leave

Every miss so far was about how many pumps. The last look is about whose. Tag each car-serving station by operator — the majors (Circle K, OKQ8, Preem, St1) against the discount automats, local independents and community-run village macks — and sort them along the same density gradient. The composition flips end to end.

It is a clean, strong gradient, not an impression: a station’s odds of being a major more than double for every tenfold rise in local density. The efficient majors build out the high-volume markets and abandon the thin tail; what keeps the countryside fuelled is a different, lower- overhead ecosystem occupying the niche they vacate.

This is a second mechanism, and worth keeping separate from the first. The 0.59 is a demand-side fact — how much provision a given catchment can support. Who owns the forecourt is a supply-side fact about market structure. The two run alongside each other; neither produces the other. The countryside is not fuelled by a different law, as the broken ruler suggested for a while. It is fuelled by different firms.

The full gradient, and the formal test — operator mix by density decile · cluster-robust logistic

Left: operator composition across ten density deciles. Right: the station-level logistic fit (odds ratio 2.13 per density decade, 95% CI 1.95–2.32, p ≈ 10⁻⁶⁴; the even-odds point sits at just 1.3 ppl/ha) with Wilson confidence bands, plus a non-parametric cross-check (Mann–Whitney: median density of majors 4.2 vs 1.6 ppl/ha, p ≈ 10⁻⁸⁰). Built on the provenance-tracked clean of the raw OSM tags described up top.

Now you try

Try to break it yourself

Here are the dials, yours to turn — the denominator (people or cars) and the box (settlement or municipality). This is the world of boxes, so the slope will wobble exactly as it did earlier, and now you know why: the settlement fails the size test, the municipality passes it. What the wobble will not do is reach the wall. Every setting stays sublinear, orbiting the catchment’s invariant 0.59, and the same towns sit stubbornly above or below the line.

Divide by
Spatial unit

The whole story

One number, twice earned

Sweden fuels its big places leaner than its small ones — β ≈ 0.6 — and the hardest attempt to break that number is the one that earned it: zoom in far enough and the law appears to split in two, until you notice the ruler shrank.

That was the whole middle of this piece. A mesh built to hold constant population gets smaller as places get denser, until the box is a fifth of a kilometre across and the station it should contain is a kilometre away. Measure people and pumps over the same ground instead, and the two regimes close back into one — the same 0.59 the municipalities gave up at the start.

Then the law earned its keep a second time, as a baseline. The places that miss it turned out to be stories, not noise: skiers in the mountains, Norwegians chasing cheaper fuel across the border, suburbs that fill up elsewhere. And running alongside the demand-side law, a supply-side one — village pumps kept alive by the operators the big brands left behind. One motorway cluster in Småland is still waiting for its traffic-count test; the lens is pointed, the data comes next.

Data. Stations: OpenStreetMap amenity=fuel (2026), deduped at 50 m. Population: SCB tätort 2023, GISCO LAU 2024. Cars: Trafikanalys 2025. Model: Poisson / Negative-Binomial GLM, log link; β tested against 1; 1,000-sample bootstrap; seed 17.

A small data study on how infrastructure scales with population in Sweden — MVP draft.
